title: Golang Wails vs Electron for a Kanban Board desktop application

tensionMap.axis: Minimize runtime footprint with a native Go backend (Wails) vs. maximize rendering consistency, ecosystem maturity, and delivery speed (Electron)
tensionMap.description: Wails ships a tiny single binary with a native Go backend but renders through each OS's own webview (WebKit / WebView2 / WebKitGTK), inheriting per-platform inconsistency and a younger ecosystem. Electron guarantees one identical Chromium everywhere plus the full npm/DnD ecosystem, at the cost of ~100-200MB bundles and higher idle memory. A Kanban board is drag-and-drop-heavy and visually animated, which raises the stakes of rendering inconsistency; it is also not very CPU-bound, which weakens the case for a native backend. Both experts have a real, structurally-opposed claim on 'product quality.'

problemDefinition.statement: Choose between Wails (Go + each OS's native webview) and Electron (bundled Chromium + Node) for building a cross-platform Kanban board desktop application.

excludes: web or mobile versions of the product

problemDefinition.successCriteria: A defensible recommendation that states the conditions under which each framework wins, backed by concrete evidence rather than reputation.

initialTopics.topic: Which framework best fits a cross-platform Kanban board, weighing footprint & native backend against rendering consistency, ecosystem, and delivery speed?
initialTopics.rationale: The core decision and the axis of the designed tension.
